cards.front: Standard ACL number ranges and what they match on.
cards.back: 1-99 (and extended 1300-1999). Match on SOURCE IP only — that's it. Used when you don't care about destination/port. Best practice: place close to the destination (otherwise you over-block on the way).

cards.front: Standard numbered ACL syntax — show 'permit anything from 10.1.1.0/24'.
cards.back:
```
access-list 10 permit 10.1.1.0 0.0.0.255
```
Apply to interface: `interface gi0/0` then `ip access-group 10 in` (or `out`). Implicit `deny any` at end.

cards.front: Wildcard mask vs subnet mask — what's the difference and how to convert?
cards.back: Wildcard: 0=match exactly, 1=ignore. Subnet mask is the OPPOSITE bitwise. Convert: 255 − each octet of subnet mask. Example: subnet 255.255.255.0 (/24) → wildcard 0.0.0.255. Subnet 255.255.255.240 (/28) → wildcard 0.0.0.15.

cards.front: What do `host` and `any` keywords mean in ACL syntax?
cards.back: `host X.X.X.X` is shorthand for `X.X.X.X 0.0.0.0` (wildcard 0 = exact match). `any` is shorthand for `0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255` (match anything). Example: `permit ip host 10.1.1.5 any`.

cards.front: What's the implicit deny in an ACL, and when does it bite you?
cards.back: Every ACL ends with implicit `deny ip any any` (or `deny any` for standard). If your ACL has only permit statements and the matched traffic isn't permitted, it's silently dropped. Always add explicit permits for legitimate traffic before applying — easy to lock yourself out of a router.

cards.front: How does an ACL process a packet — top-down or some other order?
cards.back: Strictly top-down, first match wins. Once matched, no further entries are evaluated for that packet. Ordering matters: put more specific entries before more general ones, otherwise the general entry catches everything.

cards.front: What command shows ACL hits per line and ACL contents?
cards.back: `show access-lists` (or `show access-lists 10`). Lists each entry with hit counter. Useful for verifying the ACL is matching the expected traffic. Reset counters: `clear access-list counters`.

cards.front: How many ACLs can be applied to a single interface?
cards.back: Up to TWO per interface per protocol — one inbound (`ip access-group X in`) and one outbound (`ip access-group X out`). Each direction can also have a separate IPv4 vs IPv6 ACL. Inbound is evaluated BEFORE the routing decision; outbound is evaluated AFTER routing.

cards.front: Standard NAMED ACL configuration syntax (vs numbered)?
cards.back: `ip access-list standard NAME` enters named-ACL config mode. Then add entries: `[seq] {permit|deny} <source> <wildcard>`. Sequence numbers can be inserted between existing entries (insert one at line 15 between 10 and 20) — easier to maintain than numbered ACLs. Same for extended: `ip access-list extended NAME`.

cards.front: Standard vs Extended ACL placement best practice?
cards.back: Standard: place CLOSE to the DESTINATION (matches only on source IP, so placing too early can over-block traffic to other destinations). Extended: place CLOSE to the SOURCE (matches on src+dst+port, so blocking early avoids wasting bandwidth carrying soon-to-be-dropped packets).

cards.front: What is an ACE, and how do you add a comment to an ACL?
cards.back: ACE = Access Control Entry — a single line in an ACL (one permit or deny rule). To document an ACL: `access-list 10 remark Block guest VLAN` (numbered) or `remark <text>` inside named-ACL config mode. Remarks don't process traffic — they're for humans.
